nativeRange.habitat: Generally found on coral reefs and rocky substrates with abundant crevices, overhangs, and rubble. Commonly observed congregating in groups within sheltered nooks during the day and emerging to forage at night.

decorPreferences: live rock with crevices and overhangs
caves and PVC tunnels for daytime shelter
dim or moderate lighting (species is largely nocturnal)

diet.notes: Generally an opportunistic scavenger. Has been reported to accept a wide range of foods — frozen mysis and brine shrimp, freeze-dried plankton, marine flake, sinking pellets, and meaty bits of fish or mussel. In an established reef tank, supplemental feeding once every day or two is commonly considered sufficient. Iodine, calcium, magnesium, and trace-element supplementation are generally advised to support healthy molting.

goodWith: Lysmata cleaner shrimp (L. amboinensis, L. debelius)
avoidWith: soft corals, zoanthids, mushrooms, and LPS (the shrimp will pick at polyps)
Harlequin Shrimp (Hymenocera picta) and Coral Banded Shrimp (Stenopus hispidus) — aggressive toward Camel Shrimp
large hawkfish, wrasses, triggerfish, puffers, lionfish, and moray eels (will predate the shrimp)
crabs large enough to grab a molting shrimp
any copper-based medication in the system

summary: The Camel Shrimp is a striped red-and-white marine shrimp widely sold as a starter saltwater invertebrate, and is generally considered peaceful and straightforward to keep. It is NOT reliably reef safe, however — it tends to pick at soft corals, zoanthids, and LPS polyps.
careNotes: The Camel Shrimp (Rhynchocinetes durbanensis) is a commonly available ornamental marine shrimp, frequently sold as a starter invertebrate for new saltwater keepers. It is generally considered hardy, peaceful toward fish and other shrimp, and straightforward to feed, making it a reasonable choice for beginners to saltwater keeping. CRITICAL CAVEAT: it is NOT reliably reef safe. It tends to pick at soft corals, zoanthids, mushroom anemones, and LPS polyps (large-polyp stony corals), making it a generally safe choice only for fish-only-with-live-rock (FOWLR) setups or tanks without photosynthetic corals. Reefkeepers who value their coral colonies are widely advised to avoid it. The species is also frequently misadvertised as an Aiptasia (glass anemone) control — it is not effective for that purpose; peppermint shrimp (Lysmata wurdemanni) or Berghia nudibranchs are the commonly recommended alternatives. Like all marine shrimp, the Camel Shrimp is highly sensitive to copper-based medications and to ammonia or nitrite spikes. It should only be added to a fully cycled system, drip-acclimated over one to two hours, and kept well away from any copper treatments used elsewhere in the system. The species is largely nocturnal and tends to hide during the day; providing caves, rubble piles, and overhangs gives the group secure places to cluster and reduces stress. Camel Shrimp are gregarious and are generally considered more comfortable and more active when kept in groups of two to six rather than as solitary individuals.
breedingNotes: Breeding the Camel Shrimp in captivity is generally considered very difficult, and the commercial trade is essentially entirely supplied by wild-caught specimens. Females carry between 100 and 700 eggs tucked beneath the pleopods (the small paddle-like limbs on the abdomen) for roughly 14 to 15 days before releasing free-swimming planktonic larvae. Those larvae require approximately 60 days of staged feeding — progressing through phytoplankton, rotifers, and brine shrimp nauplii — before reaching the juvenile stage. A dedicated larval rearing tank is generally required, set up without air stones or undergravel filtration that would trap the tiny larvae, and fitted with 150 μm mesh over any outlets. Mating tends to occur shortly after a female molt, when the female's shell is still soft. Males can be distinguished from females by their larger body size and proportionally longer claws.

primary.name: LiveAquaria — Camel Shrimp
primary.notes: Trade source. Provides basic husbandry parameters (temp 64-77°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.023-1.025, dKH 8-12), reef-with-caution rating, and explicit warning that the species nips at colonial anemones, disc anemones, and soft leather corals.

additional.name: Aquarium Breeder — Camel Shrimp Detailed Guide
additional.notes: Most detailed husbandry source. Substituted in after assigned secondary (Reef2Reef Wiki) returned 403 Forbidden. Provided adult size 5-6 cm, temp 22-27°C, pH 8.0-8.5, KH 8-16, lifespan 1.5-3 yr, Indo-Pacific range (South Africa, Ryukyus, Philippines, Indonesia, India), depth 2-3 m, detailed larval biology (100-700 eggs, 14-15 d incubation, 60 d larval development), copper sensitivity, and explicit reef-safety warning ('proceed at your own risk; reports of destroying reefs, picking at anemones, consuming soft corals and polyps').

additional.name: Tank Facts — Camel Shrimp
additional.notes: Cross-checked SG (1.023-1.026), temperature (76-80°F), pH (7.8-8.4), Indo-Pacific origin, social grouping recommendation of 4-6 individuals, iodine-supplementation requirement, and copper sensitivity.

additional.name: AquariumDomain — Camel Shrimp
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (2 in / ~5 cm), temperature (72-78°F), pH (8.1-8.4), SG (1.023-1.025), minimum tank (12 gal / ~45 L), 'Easy' care rating, and reef-with-caution caveats. Note: this source incorrectly lists the native range as Caribbean — the species is Indo-Pacific (Durban, South Africa is the type locality).

crustacean.moltingNotes: Like all decapod crustaceans, the Camel Shrimp molts periodically, shedding its entire shell to grow. Adults are commonly reported to molt every few weeks, though the exact interval is not well documented in hobby sources. Iodine supplementation is generally advised to support healthy, complete molts. A freshly molted individual is temporarily soft-shelled and highly vulnerable — both to tankmates that might otherwise ignore it and to cannibalism from other shrimp in the group. Providing abundant crevices and overhangs gives molting shrimp a place to hide until the new shell hardens. The shed exoskeleton can be left in the tank; the shrimp will often eat it to reclaim calcium and other minerals.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Has been bred experimentally but is not commercially captive-bred, and trade supply is essentially entirely wild-caught. Females carry between 100 and 700 eggs for roughly 14 to 15 days; the planktonic larvae that hatch require approximately 60 days of staged feeding on phytoplankton, rotifers, and brine shrimp nauplii in a dedicated larval rearing tank.
